expectations.text: Uses Reorder.Group with values and onReorder props
expectations.evidence: TodoList.tsx lines 67-73: `<Reorder.Group axis="y" values={items} onReorder={setItems} as="ul" className="todo-list__items">` — both `values` and `onReorder` props are wired correctly to state.

expectations.text: Uses Reorder.Item with key={item.id} and value={item}
expectations.evidence: TodoList.tsx line 77: `<TodoItem key={item.id} ...` (key on the rendered list element) and line 112: `<Reorder.Item value={item} as="li" ...>` — the key is on the TodoItem wrapper which renders the Reorder.Item, and `value={item}` is on Reorder.Item.

expectations.text: Uses useDragControls() and dragListener={false} to restrict drag to a handle
expectations.evidence: TodoList.tsx line 97: `const dragControls = useDragControls();` and lines 116-117: `dragListener={false}` and `dragControls={dragControls}` on Reorder.Item.

TodoList.tsx lines 143-148: the handle button has `onPointerDown={(e) => { e.preventDefault(); dragControls.start(e); }}` — calls `dragControls.start(e)` inside the onPointerDown handler (with an additional preventDefault).

expectations.text: Wraps items in AnimatePresence for enter/exit animations
expectations.evidence: TodoList.tsx line 74: `<AnimatePresence initial={false}>` wraps the mapped list of TodoItem (Reorder.Item) elements inside Reorder.Group.

expectations.text: Items have initial/animate/exit for smooth add/remove
expectations.evidence: TodoList.tsx lines 119-125 on Reorder.Item: `initial={{ opacity: 0, x: -40 }}`, `animate={{ opacity: 1, x: 0 }}`, `exit={{ opacity: 0, x: 300, transition: { duration: 0.2 } }}`.

expectations.text: For swipe-to-delete, uses drag='x' or onDragEnd with an offset threshold to remove item
expectations.evidence: TodoList.tsx line 175: `<motion.div drag="x" ...>` with `onDragEnd={handleDragEnd}`. The handler (lines 101-108) checks `Math.abs(info.offset.x) > SWIPE_THRESHOLD` (120) and calls `onRemove(item.id)`.
TodoList.tsx lines 2-8: `import { Reorder, useDragControls, motion, AnimatePresence, type PanInfo } from "motion/react";`

expectations.text: Does not use any Motion+ components
expectations.evidence: Only standard motion/react primitives are imported (Reorder, useDragControls, motion, AnimatePresence, PanInfo). No Motion+ components such as Cursor, Ticker, AnimateNumber, etc. are referenced in the file.

Output file is TodoList.tsx (.tsx extension). Includes typed interfaces (`interface Todo`, `interface TodoListProps`, `interface TodoItemProps`), typed state `useState<Todo[]>(...)`, typed handler signature `(_e: MouseEvent | TouchEvent | PointerEvent, info: PanInfo)`, and `type PanInfo` import.

claims.claim: motion/react is the React 19-compatible entry point / modern successor to framer-motion
claims.evidence: Correct — the `motion` package (imported via `motion/react`) is the rebranded successor to framer-motion and supports React 19.

claims.claim: Swipe gesture nested inside Reorder.Item works without manual gesture arbitration because Motion isolates axes (drag='x' inner, vertical outer)
claims.evidence: In practice, nesting a `drag='x'` motion.div inside a `Reorder.Item` with `dragListener={false}` can work but is not guaranteed to cleanly isolate — pointer events on the inner element can still be consumed first and block other gestures. The executor's assertion that 'no manual gesture arbitration needed' is an optimistic claim; this architecture often needs careful handling (e.g. pointer capture, stopPropagation). Not falsified by any test here, but unverified.

claims.claim: AnimatePresence + exit transform will correctly animate swipe-delete removal
claims.evidence: Reorder.Item has an `exit` prop defined and the parent is wrapped in AnimatePresence, so on removal the exit animation will run. Keys are stable (item.id), which is required for AnimatePresence to track enter/exit.

claims.claim: crypto.randomUUID() with a fallback provides stable keys
claims.evidence: Lines 28-31: uses `crypto.randomUUID()` when available, else falls back to `${Date.now()}-${Math.random().toString(36).slice(2)}`. IDs are generated once at item creation and stored, so keys remain stable.

claims.claim: Handle-only dragging follows the documented Motion pattern (dragListener=false + dragControls.start on pointer down)
claims.evidence: Matches the documented Motion reorder-with-handle pattern exactly.

suggestions.assertion: Uses Reorder.Item with key={item.id} and value={item}
suggestions.reason: The key appears on the outer TodoItem wrapper rather than directly on Reorder.Item. The current implementation is correct (React only requires the key on the top-level rendered element in the list), but a strict literal reading of the assertion (`key={item.id}` *on* Reorder.Item) could trip up graders. Consider rewording the assertion as 'each rendered list element has a stable key (e.g. item.id) and Reorder.Item receives value={item}' to avoid ambiguity.

No assertion checks that a new item is actually added to state and appears at a predictable position, or that `removeItem` is actually called from the swipe handler (vs. e.g. always firing). The current assertions check structure (props/hooks used) but not behavioral correctness. Consider an assertion like 'swipe-delete handler calls the remove function with the correct item id' or 'add handler inserts with a stable id.'

suggestions.assertion: For swipe-to-delete, uses drag='x' or onDragEnd with an offset threshold to remove item
suggestions.reason: This passes even if the threshold is absurdly low (e.g. 5px) or if the handler removes a different item. Consider tightening: 'threshold is a reasonable pixel/velocity value and the correct item id is passed to the removal callback.'

No assertion covers the nested-gesture concern: putting drag='x' inside a Reorder.Item with dragListener=false is a known tricky area. A test that the reorder handle still starts vertical reorder, and horizontal swipe starts deletion, without one cancelling the other, would be a more discriminating check.

eval_feedback.overall: All 10 assertions pass with clear evidence. The assertions cover API surface well but check structure over behavior — a component that imports the right symbols and wires the right props could still ship with broken gestures. Consider adding behavioral/correctness checks for a stronger eval.
